title: SEGMENT 8 — Polyclonal antibodies — bleeding the immunized horse

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Polyclonal antibodies — bleeding the immunized horse
Okay.
Now we shift gears.
We've spent the first half of this lecture on antibodies as biology — what they are, what they do, how the body makes them.

The second half is about antibodies as tools and as drugs — how scientists figured out how to harvest them, manufacture them, and engineer them into the most successful class of therapeutic in modern medicine.
The story of antibodies as medicine starts, surprisingly, in the 1890s, with horses and diphtheria.
In the late nineteenth century, diphtheria was a terrifying childhood disease — a bacterial infection that killed by producing a toxin that destroyed tissue in the throat and damaged the heart.
The mortality rate for severe cases was something like 30%.
Then, in 1890, a German physician named Emil von Behring made an observation that won the very first Nobel Prize in Medicine.
He noticed that if you took blood serum from an animal that had survived diphtheria and injected it into a sick patient, the patient often recovered.
There was something in the survivor's blood that could neutralize the toxin and transfer that protection to someone else.
We now know that something was — yes — antibodies.

So von Behring developed what was called diphtheria antitoxin: you'd take a horse, immunize it repeatedly with small, non-lethal doses of diphtheria toxin until the horse's immune system was producing massive quantities of anti-toxin antibodies.
Then you'd bleed the horse, collect its serum, partially purify it, and give it to sick children.
It worked.
Mortality from diphtheria plummeted in places where antitoxin was available.
The era of antibody therapy had begun, and it had begun with horses.
Now, what you got from that horse, when you bled it, was something we now call polyclonal antibodies.
And I want to use the analogy carefully here, because the polyclonal-versus-monoclonal distinction is going to govern everything that happens next.
Here's the analogy.
You go to a locksmith, and you ask for keys that will open a particular lock.
The locksmith works by hand.
She looks at the lock, makes a key, tries it.
Makes another, tries it.
Over the course of an afternoon, she produces dozens of keys, all roughly the same — they all open the lock — but each one is slightly different, because they were each cut by hand.
Some grip the lock tightly, some loosely.
Some catch on the third pin, some on the fifth.
All of them work, but they're a mixed bag — they're a population of similar-but-not-identical keys, hand-cut, varied.
That's a polyclonal antibody preparation.

It's an immune horse's whole serum, containing antibodies from many different B cell clones, all of which happen to bind to diphtheria toxin, but at slightly different epitopes, with slightly different affinities, and with slightly different downstream behaviors.
Now contrast that with a different way to make keys.
You go to a key factory.
They look at the lock, design the perfect key for it, and then run off ten thousand absolutely identical, machine-pressed, factory-uniform copies.
Every key is interchangeable with every other.
That's a monoclonal antibody preparation, and we'll get to how to make those in the next segment.
So polyclonal is a locksmith's mixed bag; monoclonal is factory-pressed identical keys.
Both can open the lock.
They have very different properties as products.
What are the advantages of polyclonal?
Well, the mixed-bag nature is actually useful for some applications.

Because polyclonal antibodies recognize many different epitopes on the same target, they're robust — if the target has a mutation that eliminates one epitope, there are still antibodies in the mix that bind elsewhere.
They tend to give stronger signals in lab assays, because multiple antibodies can coat the same target simultaneously.
And they're cheap and fast to produce — you just immunize an animal and bleed it.
What are the disadvantages?
Three big ones.
First, batch-to-batch variability is enormous.
The polyclonal antibodies you get from this rabbit today are not the same as the polyclonal antibodies you'll get from this rabbit next month, or from a different rabbit altogether.
Every batch is genuinely different.
For research, that's annoying.
For pharmaceutical use, it's a regulatory nightmare.
Second, you're depending on a live animal, which limits the supply.
Third, and most importantly for therapy: a horse antibody is foreign to a human patient.
The human immune system recognizes it as foreign — it's a horse protein — and mounts an immune response against it.
So the first dose works; the second dose can cause a dangerous allergic reaction called serum sickness.
This was a real problem in the early days of antitoxin therapy.
For a long time, despite these problems, polyclonal antibodies from immunized animals were the only game in town.
They saved lives, but they were a blunt and unreliable instrument.
The dream — and it was a dream people had for decades — was to find a way to produce unlimited quantities of a single, specific, perfectly characterized antibody.
To go from the locksmith to the factory.
That dream sat unfulfilled until 1975.
